topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white on faded parchment, lay crumpled on my desk. "Effective Spring Semester 2025, [College Name] will cease operations." I didn't even bother to read the fine print. My hands were shaking so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady myself. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound that felt rusty and unused. I slammed my fist down, rattling the cheap ceramic mug beside it. "This is a joke," I hissed, the sound thin and brittle. I paced the cramped apartment, the floorboards groaning under my frantic steps. Every corner of the room seemed to pulse with a furious energy that wouldn't subside. I kicked a stack of outdated textbooks across the room, the noise echoing sharply in the silence of the late afternoon.

[story 2]

I stared at the campus map, tracing the lines of the buildings with a finger until the paper tore. It wasn't just a building closing; it was the demolition of everything I had painstakingly built. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I stood by the window, watching the sunset paint the sky in mocking hues of orange and purple. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles protesting beneath my skin. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. I turned abruptly, pacing the length of the living room in tight, sharp strides. I started throwing objects—a remote, a cushion—against the wall, not with real force, but with a desperate, jerky violence that seemed to drain the tension building inside me.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended ten minutes ago, leaving a ringing silence that was deafening. I walked into the common area, my breathing shallow and rapid. The air felt thick, suffocating. I looked around at the empty chairs, the silent monuments to shared ambitions that were now dissolving into dust. A sharp, involuntary laugh escaped me, quickly swallowed by a harsh exhale. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the frantic energy vibrating just beneath my scalp. "No," I whispered, my voice cracking with sheer disbelief. I began to pace in a tight circle, my movements jerky and unpredictable, my eyes darting everywhere and nowhere, searching for an explanation that simply wasn't there. The frustration coiled in my chest, a tight, painful knot that demanded release.
